figures.caption: Fig. 3. Comparison between the simulated (top), ML predicted (middle), and residual (bottom) results for the electric field in Case 2, at t = 50 (left column), and t = 100 (right column).

Fig. 7. Simulated and predicted solutions for Case 2, with the initial condition and two time steps t = 50 and t = 100. The material to the right of the interface (dotted line) has a random speed of light that must be learned from training data.

Fig. 9. Case 2 predicted solution relative error for t ∈ [0, 100], mean value (dark red line) and standard deviation (shaded region) across all test data. The error grows approximately linearly in time.
Fig. 4. Simulated and predicted solutions for Case 1, at two time steps t = 100 and t = 200. The dotted line at xj = 0.5 is the material interface where the speed of light changes.

Fig. 6. Case 1 predicted solution relative error over time steps t = [0, 200], with the mean value (dark red line) and standard deviation (shaded region) across all test cases. Time step t = 100 is approximately when the wave pulse is crossing the material boundary.
